source_paper_title: Risk-Averse Markov Decision Processes Through a Distributional Lens
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Cheng_2025, title={Risk-Averse Markov Decision Processes Through a Distributional Lens}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0211}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0211}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Cheng, Ziteng and Jaimungal, Sebastian}, year={2025}, month=aug, pages={1707–1733} }
context:
Page 14, Remark 2.12 (problem formulation discussion)

Another potentially promising approach to address uncertainty in the discount factor \(\gamma\) involves considering the limit of \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C(X,A))\) as \(\gamma \to 1\). In the risk-neutral setting with discrete state and action spaces, it has been shown that this limit while scaled properly coincides with average optimality (cf. [51, Corollary 8.2.5], [30]). However, the equivalence of this limit in current context remains unclear.
title: Equivalence between discount-to-one limit and average optimality in risk-averse MDPs
background_latex:
Consider a discrete-time controlled Markov process with state space \(X\) and action space \(A\), with (possibly randomized) Markov policies \(p=(\pi_t)_{t\in\mathbb{N}}\), where each \(\pi_t(x)\) is a probability measure on \(A\) supported on an admissible set \(A_t(x)\subseteq A\). Let \(P(t,x,a,\cdot)\) be a transition kernel on \(X\), and let \(C_t(x,a,x')\in\mathbb{R}\) be a bounded one-step cost. 

Fix a discount factor \(\gamma\in(0,1)\). A (time- and state-dependent) law-invariant convex risk measure at the distributional level is a functional \(\sigma_{t,x}\) mapping the law \(\mu\) of a bounded real random variable to a value in \(( -\infty,\infty]\), satisfying (i) translation invariance \(\sigma_{t,x}(\mu*\delta_c)=\sigma_{t,x}(\mu)+c\), (ii) monotonicity with respect to first-order stochastic dominance, and (iii) convexity with respect to mixtures (as formalized on probability measures). A dynamic risk measure (DRM) \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\) is constructed by nesting/composing the one-step risk mappings \(\sigma_{t,X_t}\) applied to regular conditional distributions of future (discounted) cost streams under a controlled process \((X,A)\).

For each \(\gamma\in(0,1)\), define the infinite-horizon discounted risk-averse performance criterion (value) under an admissible policy (or controlled process) as \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\). In classical risk-neutral MDPs with finite state/action spaces, the scaled limit \((1-\gamma)\, \mathbb{E}[\sum_{t\ge 0} \gamma^t C_t]\) as \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) coincides with the optimal long-run average cost under suitable conditions; the paper highlights that an analogous statement for the present risk-averse DRM framework is not established.
problem_definition_latex:
Establish conditions under which the discount-to-one limit of the discounted infinite-horizon distributional dynamic risk measure recovers an average-optimality criterion. Concretely, analyze whether there is an average-cost (or average-risk) optimality notion \(\mathrm{Avg}(C)\) for the risk-averse MDP such that, for appropriate scaling and under suitable assumptions,
\[
\lim_{\gamma\uparrow 1}\, (1-\gamma)\, \inf_{(X,A)\in\Psi}\, \varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C) 
\quad\text{equals}\quad
\inf_{\text{admissible policies}}\, \mathrm{Avg}(C),
\]
or otherwise characterize the limit \(\lim_{\gamma\uparrow 1} \varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\) (possibly after scaling) and its relationship to an average optimality objective in this risk-averse setting.

A (time- and state-dependent) law-invariant convex risk measure at the distributional level is a functional \(\sigma_{t,x}\) mapping the law \(\mu\) of a bounded real random variable to a value in \(( -\infty,\infty]\), satisfying (i) translation invariance \(\sigma_{t,x}(\mu*\delta_c)=\sigma_{t,x}(\mu)+c\), (ii) monotonicity with respect to first-order stochastic dominance, and (iii) convexity with respect to mixtures (as formalized on probability measures). Establish conditions under which the discount-to-one limit of the discounted infinite-horizon distributional dynamic risk measure recovers an average-optimality criterion. Concretely, analyze whether there is an average-cost (or average-risk) optimality notion \(\mathrm{Avg}(C)\) for the risk-averse MDP such that, for appropriate scaling and under suitable assumptions,
\[

\]

open_problem_quote: Another potentially promising approach to address uncertainty in the discount factor \(\gamma\) involves considering the limit of \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C(X,A))\) as \(\gamma \to 1\). In the risk-neutral setting with discrete state and action spaces, it has been shown that this limit while scaled properly coincides with average optimality (cf. [51, Corollary 8.2.5], [30]). However, the equivalence of this limit in current context remains unclear.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can search over small finite-state/action MDPs and specific law-invariant convex risk measures (e.g., entropic or CVaR-based nested risk) to numerically test whether a proposed scaling limit matches a candidate average-cost notion. However, the space of admissible DRMs and technical conditions is broad, and failures may depend on subtle regularity assumptions, making brute-force falsification only moderately feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary code loop could parameterize finite MDP instances and families of state-dependent risk measures, then optimize policies and estimate \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}\) for \(\gamma\) near 1 to detect convergence patterns and hypothesize correct scaling/average criteria. The fitness signal (numerical discrepancy between scaled discounted values and candidate average-risk values) is computable, but expensive and potentially noisy due to near-unit discounting and nested risk evaluation.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The source paper (Cheng–Jaimungal) constructs discounted infinite-horizon distributional DRMs \(\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\) by nesting state-dependent law-invariant convex risk measures \(\sigma_{t,x}\) applied to regular conditional laws of \(C_t+\gamma\,\text{future}\). Under weak continuity of the transition kernel and lower semicontinuity/Fatou-type regularity of \(\sigma_{t,x}\), it establishes finite- and infinite-horizon DPPs and existence of optimal Markov policies for each fixed \(\gamma<1\). It also explicitly highlights that, unlike the risk-neutral case, an Abelian/Tauberian link between the \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) discounted problem (possibly scaled by \(1-\gamma\)) and an average-cost/average-risk criterion is not known in this nonlinear, time-consistent risk setting.

Prior work by Ruszczyński provides the canonical recursive risk-averse dynamic programming framework via risk transition mappings, while Bäuerle–Glauner develop related discounted DPP theory for recursive risk measures and conditions for deterministic optimal actions. These works clarify that time consistency alone yields a Bellman recursion but does not supply the linear structure used in classical proofs of discount-to-average equivalence (e.g., additive Poisson equations and affine contraction arguments). On the average-criterion side, Shen–Stannat–Obermayer treat risk-sensitive Markov control processes including average dynamic risk measures under strong regularity (e.g., strong Feller-type) assumptions, thereby offering candidate definitions of \(\mathrm{Avg}(C)\) in a time-consistent risk-averse framework; however, they do not prove that \((1-\gamma)\inf\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}(C)\) converges to that average criterion as \(\gamma\uparrow 1\), nor do they address the distributional/state-dependent \(\sigma_{t,x}\) formulation.

Recent forward citations focus on robust/time-consistent recursion and finite-horizon robust RL with nested distortion risks; they provide tools for recursion and worst-case quantile calculations but do not engage with infinite-horizon \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) asymptotics. Overall, the discount-to-one versus average-risk equivalence remains open for the distributional DRM setting. Promising directions include: (i) imposing stationarity and ergodicity/unichain conditions plus additional structure on \(\sigma\) (e.g., coherent/spectral forms admitting dual representations) to derive an additive eigenvalue problem \(h+\lambda=\mathcal{T}h\) for a nonlinear Bellman operator \(\mathcal{T}\); (ii) developing nonlinear Tauberian theorems for nested risk operators, possibly via subadditivity/positive homogeneity and span seminorm contractions; and (iii) identifying counterexamples where nonlinearity breaks the Abelian limit, to delineate the sharp assumptions needed.

sources.title: Uncertainty propagation and dynamic robust risk measures
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper develops a finite-horizon framework of dynamic uncertainty sets \(u=\{u_t\}\) and associated dynamic robust risk measures \(R^{u,\rho}\) defined by one-step robustification \(R^{u,\rho}_{t,s}(X_{t+1:s})=\operatorname*{ess\,sup}\{\rho_t(Y):Y\in u_{t+1}(X_{t+1:s})\}\). Its main structural theorem (Theorem 5) gives a recursive representation: \(R\) is weak recursive iff there exists a static uncertainty set \(u^{\varsigma}\) such that for all \(t\),
\[R_{t,T}(X_{t+1:T})=R^{u^{\varsigma}}_t\bigl(Y_{t+1}+R^{u^{\varsigma}}_{t+1}(Y_{t+2}+\cdots+R^{u^{\varsigma}}_{T-1}(Y_T)\cdots)\bigr),\]
with \(Y_t=X_t-R^{u^{\varsigma}}_t(0)\); and if additionally normalised then this yields strong time-consistency (a robust analogue of the Cheridito–Delbaen–Kupper recursion).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns the \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) (discount-to-one) limit of infinite-horizon discounted, distributional, law-invariant convex dynamic risk measures in risk-averse MDPs and its relationship to an average-cost/average-risk criterion. This citing paper is finite-horizon and focuses on axiomatic/time-consistency and recursive representations of robust dynamic risk measures built from uncertainty sets; it does not study Markov decision processes, stationary control, infinite-horizon limits, or any Abelian/Tauberian link between discounted and average criteria. At most, its recursion/time-consistency results could be used as technical tools when defining well-posed dynamic risk criteria under model uncertainty, but it provides no results about \((1-\gamma)\varsigma^{X,\gamma}_{0,\infty}\) as \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) or average optimality.

sources.title: Robust reinforcement learning with dynamic distortion risk measures
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper develops a finite-horizon (
0T<\infty\)) robust risk-aware RL framework for strongly time-consistent dynamic risk measures built by nesting one-step conditional robust distortion risk measures. Key technical results include explicit characterizations of the worst-case conditional quantile in a conditional 2-Wasserstein ball: for uncertainty set \(\{Z^\varphi: \|\breve F_t-\breve F_{\varphi,t}\|_2\le \epsilon_s\}\), \(\breve F^*_{\varphi,t}(\cdot\mid s,a)=\big(\breve F_{\theta,t}(\cdot\mid s,a)+\gamma_s(\cdot)/(2\lambda^*)\big)^{\uparrow}\) with \(\|\breve F^*_{\varphi,t}-\breve F_{\theta,t}\|_2=\epsilon_s\) (Thm 3.2); and with additional moment constraints (Eq. (2.2b)) an explicit affine-in-\(\breve F_{\theta,t}\) formula for \(\breve F^*_{\varphi,t}\) (Thm 3.5), plus a deterministic policy-gradient formula for the resulting robust dynamic objective (Thm 3.6).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns the \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) discount-to-one limit and its equivalence (after scaling) to an average-cost/average-risk criterion for infinite-horizon risk-averse MDPs under distributional dynamic risk measures. This citing paper does not study infinite-horizon discounted criteria, does not analyze \(\gamma\uparrow 1\) limits, and does not define or relate to an average-risk optimality notion. Its \(\gamma\) denotes a distortion function (not the discount factor), and the setting is episodic finite-horizon RL with nested one-step conditional (robust) distortion risk measures. Thus it does not resolve the discount-to-average equivalence question; at most it provides related methodology (quantile/Wasserstein robustification, time-consistent nesting) that could be ingredients in a future average-risk analysis.
